item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This was not intended as Roleplay…

Next time, make it clearer that you're joking. Writer intent doesn't always translate directly to reader perception.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Seriously?
…
I was just trying to have fun with this Meta SCP
with 2 Memes…
This was not intended as Roleplay…

…

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Tarkin, do not roleplay in the forums. It's against the rules of the site.

As stated in the Guide for Newbies, which you should have read already:

Don't pretend the articles or stories on the wiki are real. Yes, some characters in articles have the same name as writers here (as the characters are author avatars). But we don't roleplay or pretend we’re these characters on the forums. Everything on the site and in the chat is out-of-character, which means that you shouldn’t roleplay or pretend that you are a character in the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [REDACTED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wanted that ambiguity in SCP-3519 too. Ideally it shouldn't be exactly clear what's really anomolous and how many people are actually affected by the meme or just died because of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fascinating question occurs; how can The Foundation tell the difference between memetic anomalies and mundane propagation of a meme?

This article and a few others imply that they must have a test, screening protocol, or at least a list of criteria maintained by the Memetics Division. Something that a scientist can point to as definitive when they say "Rickrolling - not a skip. Nazism - wish it was, but not a skip. Lizstomania - we thought it was, but we were wrong. Overzealous redacting - that's not just training gaps and petty bureaucrat power flexing, it's a skip!"

Which isn't a critique. I like this article. Partially because it illuminates an angle I hadn't thought of before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is also a very good chance the Foundation is just redacting random shit to throw you further off from the truth. Kinda like an intentional "boy who cried wolf".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would explain why there are so many apparently arbitrary "[REDACTED]" and similar all over the wiki about SCP details that would seem harmless. Makes you wonder if what was behind the blacking was actually important or just a side effect of this SCP. So far, whenever you saw one of those you'd just have to use your imagination and think of "something bad", but this SCP makes you think "…or is it?"

item.title: Re: But Canonicity
item.content:encoded: It really doesn't say that at all. UNNECESSARILY censored information MAY be caused by SCP-1059, as 1059 is an infection that produces that effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, it's a short but well written SCP about a concept that I haven't seen before. Another part that I appreciate is that this entry lacks the ridiculously expositional style of writing that I've found with the other "In-universe explains something that happens out of universe" articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree with some of the comments saying that this should be a -J, or that it hurts the canon. Sure the meta tongue-in-cheek is hilarious and speaks to a very real issue (seeing a redaction in the containment procedures makes my skin crawl) but it also works within the confines of the SCP universe. This would be a very real problem for the Foundation, just as it is for us as readers. Frankly, I love it. I +1'ed as soon as I realized what was going on in the first couple paragraphs.

item.title: major security risk/leak being disregarded
item.content:encoded: wouldn't the existence of Document SCP-1059-A be an infectious source and therefore a memetic agent that obviously compromised and will continue to compromise any and all reader

item.title: But Canonicity
item.content:encoded: But this scp completely changes every redacted/obscured information. It means that every single censored information is henceforth compromised by 1059

item.title: Just a few odd questions...
item.content:encoded:
How much information is is needed for the anomaly to activate? Is a sentence, a phrase, a word or even just a single letter enough to trigger it?

How do they test to see if an individual has been cured of it, do they give them a normal document and then see what they write.

Does this anomaly affect a person's ability to draw in which they leave out or obscure certain details of a drawing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What this make me wonder is, what's behind the [redacted] Procedure 110-Montauk
Yeeaahh!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [UPVOTE EXPUNGED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Methodologies are memes too, and can be spread via observation. In a sense it could be called a "nega-meme," but that would still be a form of meme. One of the problems (and strengths) with memes as a conceptual tool is that everything is a meme, in every way you can take the statement:
"Everything" is a meme. "Every thing" is a meme. Every "thing" is a meme. etc.
Memes are memes. Logical proofs that memes do not exist are memes. Bees are memes (except when they are bees). Memes are not bees (except when they are). Bees are bees.

But seriously, you're right. Memetics is most useful when looking at the adaptations and interactions between memes and their parallels with biological evolution. This is like a parasitic vine choking its host trees to death.

Vines are bees…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I find interesting about this is that it serves to limit the spread of information. By spreading via the documents it affects, it must be spread by the particular manner in which information is absent. This isn't a meme, it's a nega-meme.

item.title: Whoa! This SCP is real... er... "real"
item.content:encoded:
I promised myself not to stay up late commenting, but I love the Matryoshka doll of Fridge logic here. My first thought was,"eh, funny and meta." Then, immediately, came, "In setting, this would be dangerous and it makes a lot of sense, I mean people are doing this on the site, and what was that thing I read about…"

And it comes to this: This is a meme. A real meme. A well documented, but ill understood meme. According to some article I can't be troubled to hunt down at 2am, but think I read about 6 months ago, this is a major issue in the Intelligence community and has actually cost lives. This, being real, serves as an excellent example of a meme deserving of containment. The idea of over classification/expungement/redaction is transmitted when a new person begins to see enough of it that overuse becomes the exemplar and they begin acting "appropriately." These behaviors can be comically excessive and slow to unlearn. I've heard that at least one IRL memo on how to assess the appropriate classification level of documents was itself classified over halfway up the scale of clearance levels (making the memo nearly useless in context).

So…the memetic effect is real (or close enough), not understood, and hard to contain. Even if it is real, it should have a designation and a containment effort. Unchecked, half the SCP files would contain vital information requiring level 4 or 5 clearance, or would fail to fully explain the anomalous object (leaving personnel to understand the SCP later via "fridge logic").
Oh… right…

BTW, I love good fridge logic, but sometimes giving containment personnel fair warning seems more reasonable, in setting.
It's funny because it's like it's making fun of new people who do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To tell the truth, I didn't completely read your post(I am human)
I just saw " this needs to be a j". And assumed based on that. Still though my comment was more focused on Ihpkmn's post in which he was tired of people making such assumptions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you were paying attention, you'd notice that I didn't say this article needs to be a -J "because it's funny" - on the contrary, I thought I made it clear that I was aware that that is not what qualifies a -J. Perhaps I wasn't as clear as I thought. What I meant was that it should be a -J because it's a parody of something that does actually happen - as you yourself agreed, it's a reference to a thing that does actually happen on this site.

The Foundation's motto is "Secure, Contain, and Protect", but it does those things to anomalous items/events/etc., not to the general public. The Foundation's purpose is to "Secure, Contain, and Protect" things that would throw the general concepts of what is "normal" and "possible" completely into chaos. This scip is a thing that is explainable by modern science, psychology, etc. - yes, newbies don't read the guides, that's a perfectly rational scientific explanation for this "phenomenon". Therefore, this doesn't qualify as a "mainlist" scip.

That's exactly my point: Every article on this site is a work of fiction; this scip is not. This article is about a thing that does actually happen, which means it's not something the Foundation would need to contain, because it's real. I'm really not sure how to be clearer about this…If I'm still not making my point clear, would someone who speaks normal human please translate my argument for me? I'd really appreciate it. Please and thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although that comment was harsh, I can't agree any more with your points; there is no mainlister SCP that needs to be a -j, in fact, there are several SCPs that would make great mainlisters, (1417-j is what comes to mind) there is no definitive criteria for an article to absolutely be a j.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This needs to be a -J, specifically because it's a real-life thing that's also funny because it mainly refers to stuff that happens on this particular site…

Aha. Ahahahaa. Hahahaa. No.

Just because it's meta and funny doesn't make it a -J. If that was the case, then SCP-426 would be a -J. SCP-732 would be a -J. SCP-1839 would be a -J. Funny does not mean it is not SCP Worthy, so long as it is interesting.

It contains things to preserve normalcy, not necessarily to keep everyone safe.

Oh, so the Foundation's Motto isn't Secure, Contain, Protect? What is it then? Secure, Contain, Piss-Around?

This is a real memetic agent that really afflicts people on this site - that makes it real enough that it must be explainable by modern science, which means it's not an actual scip.

…how is it real? Everything on the site is fictional. Yes, some people, usually newbies, overuse censorship and blackboxes, and from what I can tell, this article was made specifically to point this out, while still standing alone by having an interesting effect and story. As for it being explainable, here's an explanation: Newbies don't read the guides.

I swear, if I see one more post that says "Article X should be an SCP-J because reason Y", I am going to burn something down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This needs to be a -J, specifically because it's a real-life thing that's also funny because it mainly refers to stuff that happens on this particular site…It's a lot like a -J I've been working on myself in that regard, actually - I may even cross-reference them when (if) I publish it, it feels like that would really work here. Anyway, yes, it would be a dangerous thing if it was a real memetic agent that affected real organizations, but also keep in mind what the Foundation actually does: It contains things to preserve normalcy, not necessarily to keep everyone safe. This is a real memetic agent that really afflicts people on this site - that makes it real enough that it must be explainable by modern science, which means it's not an actual scip.

I'm going to upvote it anyway because…well, it's just a thing that calls for an upvote - it does belong on this site, and I have nothing but respect for whoever wrote it. I'm just saying, it's in the wrong place, and I feel a lot more confident in saying that here than I have ever felt saying those words on this site before - I'm not saying it just because this article is funny, I'm saying it because this article is a parody and a jab at a real-life occurrence that's commonly seen on this very website. So, yeah; upvoted, but please move it. Please and thank you. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As said by others prior, I really think this one doesn't belong on the main list. There's nothing that particularly distinguishes this 'memetic agent' from mere foolish paranoia. As it is, it makes me chuckle, and I'd be voting up if it was a -J.

But I'll abstain instead.
oh, and also…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way to solve the issue is so mundane and straightforward. But, right beside it, you see the version covered in black boxes and [REDACTED] and wonder… How in heck do you get cured of it? Death? A wet Willie? 110-Montauk?

I was thinking heavy exposure to Streissand Effect would do the trick

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
germany is infected.
all of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
someone managed to make the [REDACTED]'s semi-paranoia fuel, and meta.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those articles you assume it's somewhere on the site, because it's such an easy idea, that you just haven't found yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Punch that shark, punch it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The NSA is a real organization while SCP are fake. Possibly some idiot might look for Agent Jameson or at least records of one.
Not that I'm complaining.

Also related and possibly funny (as humor is subjective):
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1459-j

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So all of that poor expungement in new articles are merely new staff infected with SCP-1059? It all makes sense now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's everywhere. These is NO ESCAPE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like a well-done meta article, and this qualifies. The snark is appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't write SPC's, son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… hm. Not funny enough for a -J, but I don't think the meta itself works as a serious SPC.
I may not be an expert, and still trying to understand memetics and all, but don't those have a 'vector'? A way to transmit themselves?
If that's so, how does this one transmit exactly? Is seeing the censored articles? 'cause in that case, having the previous version there is inviting people to get infected.
Or it just appears outta nowhere on someone with those tasks? 'cause in that case isn't memetic… right?
I think I have to read it again and check before voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this isnt funny enough to be a -J. and while it's meta, it does stand well just on its own.

the only real objection to this i feel is that, in a way, it is an attempt at moving toward "canon", by granting legitimacy to those articles where people expunged information inappropriately, giving an "in character" explanation for that phenomenon.

outside of that, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like a freaking paragon for joke SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In regards to your collapsable bit, I'm curious what you thought about SCP-1983?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually enjoyed this article. It would be perfect as a -J, but I honestly think it's just an excuse to cover for all the articles with overdone redactions, expungements, and those evil, evil black boxes. It's well written, an well executed. It's really the core idea I have to wonder about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boo-hoo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… wow. You know, after reading that I have to say… it's highly possible. I'd enjoy reading that tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Stands up in the support group circle* Hi. My name is Chazz, and I'm a fan of meta-articles… B-but, only well written ones..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm so torn.

On the one hand, everyone's going to like this because it's timely and scathing and a great example of how to be meta.

On the other hand, you should be ashamed of yourself. Only I'm not sure if that's a legitimate shame or the shame of being good.

Whatever, it's hilarious, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fucking love meta articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, I thought this was kind of goofy. Not long after, the Fridge Horror kicked in. Not long after that, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…oh god the title please don't let it be what I think it is please don't let it be it's at +15 so far so surely it can't be what I think it is alright I'm gonna read it now oh god oh god it is exactly what I thought it would be oh well at least it didn't use that stupid thing with another version of the document to redact everything out of itself oh wait there is a collapsible I wonder what's in it oh no oh god oh god WHY?!!

Seriously, through.

A year ago, when the only "meta" (not in traditional, but in described in tag guide sense) articles were The Toaster and The Pope and The Plague and The Bowl, I could've maybe possibly upvoted this. Actually, no, four times is stretching it already.

Now we have The Printer and The Minotaur and I think there were more in 1000+ block.

And, another thing. The "there is this whole different version of scprocedures and description in the collapsible" thing. Zachary did it, and it was mostly alright. Then Bugsy did it, and it was done clumsy in my opinion, but I forgive Bugsy because he is so cute I just want to hug him. Now, I really don't want to see anything else try to do it again. Two times was more than enough, thank you. It is formulaic, predictable, and, once you've seen it one time, really not interesting anymore.

So, back to this article. It have the overdone "meta" thing, overdone "another document" thing, and memetics, which are totally new and interesting.

Also, I knew every word of this article after reading the title. Every damn word. That shouldn't happen, ever.

And maybe I am just bitter because once upon a time every page I've read here held a wonder of new idea, sick, twisted idea that I could've never read anywhere else, and which would never come into my head on its own. And maybe that's just a natural evolution of the site, from few precious gems into the field of glass shards. And if that's the case, I'll go and write a skip about a sphere that liquidizes the bones of everyone in a kilometer radius, because that's a very dangerous thing that should obviously be contained by the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This nicely straddles the border between meta and straight-laced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this gives some insight into how the people who've been doing this job for years might function. They see excessive censorship and go all "Is this memetic how do we contain it oh god". They probably flip their shit over things that later turn out to be completely normal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd consider upvoting it just for the mental image of Sorts the character (memetic specialist as he is) hearing of it and and weeping.

Though honestly, my initial thought on it was "Don't attribute to an SCP what can be attributed to simple human stupidity". It just feels like, as a meta thing, many similar things have been done before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the contrast between the official report and the one affected by 1059. The way to solve the issue is so mundane and straightforward. But, right beside it, you see the version covered in black boxes and [REDACTED] and wonder… How in heck do you get cured of it? Death? A wet Willie? 110-Montauk?

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least, now we know where 579 comes from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would have loved this when I was still lurking, just reading entries.
Now that I'm behind the curtain, watching the actors prepare?
I fucking hate meta articles.
But that is only personal taste.
Well written, well done, just not my cuppa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say that signs on bathroom doors are an example of a real memetic compulsion, yeah. The urge to comply is something provided by society itself, not the signs, but that is how this stuff works. You see the signs, you know which room you are supposed to use. If you don't know the meaning of the signs or weren't already taught that sneaking into the ladies' room is against the rules you wouldn't be subject to the compulsion. And how much of a compulsion you actually feel depends on your personality.

edit: no I am not going to write this up no one should write this up! :E

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what, you're right, this would make a brilliant -J

And… and then D-121iu1o2192i will sneak into the Women's room instead and he will be vivisected to discover how he is immune to the memetic compulsion…

And the author would be using the phrase "memetic compulsion" in an entirely correct sense, assuming that I'm understanding memetics properly THIS TIME AROUND.

…Sorts, you should go and write that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Expand on this, and make it a tale. DOO EET!
